dyed_lifted_polyps: A raised, reddened lesion with irregular borders mimicking dye-enhanced polyps but caused by localized mucosal inflammation and hyperemia rather than neoplastic growth.
An elevated mucosal fold with superficial blood vessels resembling dye-stained polyps, yet lacking the uniform coloration and distinct stalk characteristic of true neoplastic polyps.
A cluster of small, closely grouped lymphoid follicles appearing as dyed, lifted polyps due to their rounded contours but lacking the chromatic intensity of dye application.
A submucosal mass with smooth, dome-shaped elevation falsely resembling a dyed polyp; its pale, translucent surface contrasts with the vivid pigmentation of genuine dye-marked lesions.
A healing ulcer bed with granulation tissue mimicking a dyed polyp’s appearance; irregular surface texture and surrounding erythema create confusion despite absent neoplastic features.
dyed_resection_margins: Hyperplastic polyps with indigo carmine dye application mimicking resection margins due to similar irregular borders and staining patterns.
Inflammatory bowel disease-associated ulcers with surrounding erythema resembling dyed margins from post-resection staining techniques.
Superficial mucosal tears with blood oozing that appears as linear markings similar to tattoo dye markings at resection sites.
Sessile serrated adenomas with subtle margin elevation post-dye spray, confusingly resembling non-lifting margins.
Post-biopsy ulcerations with fibrin deposits creating irregular outlines that mimic the appearance of dyed resection margins.
esophagitis: Reddened, inflamed gastric mucosa with scattered erosions in the antrum region, often mistaken for esophagitis due to similar erythematous appearance but located in the stomach.
Linear tears and ulcers in the proximal esophagus with minimal surrounding inflammation, resembling esophagitis but caused by mechanical trauma from vomiting or retching episodes.
White plaques and pseudomembranes covering the esophageal lining, mimicking esophagitis-associated exudates but indicative of fungal infection or antibiotic-associated colitis.
Granular, nodular appearance of the lower esophagus with interspersed red spots, simulating chronic esophagitis but associated with Barrett’s metaplasia.
Diffuse vascular congestion and mosaic pattern in the distal esophagus without ulcerations, resembling non-erosive esophagitis but linked to portal hypertension.
normal_cecum: A cecal segment with mild erythema and scattered aphthous ulcers, resembling normal vascular patterns but indicating early inflammatory bowel disease.
A slightly dilated cecal lumen with adherent mucoid debris, mimicking normal mucus layers but suggesting luminal pathology or infection.
A cecal wall with focal thickening and irregular nodularity, appearing similar to normal anatomical folds but indicative of neoplastic or inflammatory changes.
A pale, edematous cecal mucosa with patchy granularity, resembling normal variation but hinting at ischemic or infiltrative disease.
A cecal diverticulum-like outpouching with surrounding hyperemia, visually confused with normal cecal anatomy but representing acquired structural abnormality.
normal_pylorus: A round, smooth opening with pale pink mucosa resembling the pylorus but surrounded by irregular, erythematous borders mimicking chronic gastritis rather than healthy tissue.
A tubular structure with concentric folds and a central aperture resembling pyloric anatomy but exhibiting abnormal granularity and scattered erosions indicative of Crohn’s disease.
A funnel-shaped lumen with pale mucosa and faint vascular patterns resembling the pylorus but displaying asymmetric narrowing and ulcerated margins suggestive of malignancy.
A circular orifice with smooth edges and light brownish hue mimicking the pylorus but featuring overlying fibrin exudate and peripheral edema consistent with peptic ulceration.
A slightly dilated channel with longitudinal ridges and mucus-covered lining resembling pyloric anatomy but exhibiting abnormal tortuosity and variceal patterns indicative of portal hypertension.
normal_z_line: A jagged, erythematous mucosal transition resembling a Z-line but with irregular surface granularity and subtle ulcerations at the gastroesophageal junction, mimicking inflammation rather than normal anatomy.

A smooth, pale pink epithelial boundary extending slightly above the Z-line with scattered intestinal metaplasia features, closely resembling Barrett’s esophagus rather than a standard squamocolumnar junction.
A linear, whitish-veined structure at the gastroesophageal junction with fine vascular patterns, mistaken for a Z-line but indicative of early neoplastic changes like low-grade dysplasia.
A Z-line mimic with clustered small erosions and telangiectasias creating a pseudo-margin, simulating normal anatomical contrast but representing portal hypertensive gastropathy.
A slightly elevated, irregular Z-line-like ridge with adherent mucus and erythematous borders, resembling gastric antral ectopy where columnar epithelium extends abnormally into the distal esophagus.
polyps: A cluster of translucent, mucus-covered folds resembling a sessile polyp but lacking vascular patterns and displaying irregular, fluid-filled creases.
A dark, rounded foreign body partially embedded in mucosa, mimicking a bleeding polyp but with jagged edges and uneven texture from external debris.
A pedunculated structure composed of intertwined blood vessels and fibrous tissue, falsely resembling a polyp stalk but lacking a distinct head or epithelial surface.
A localized area of inflamed mucosa with granular texture and erythematous borders, simulating a hyperplastic polyp but with diffuse swelling and no discrete mass.
A reflection artifact from endoscopic lighting creating a false spherical lesion with sharp margins and uniform color, lacking the irregular surface of a true polyp.
ulcerative_colitis: **Patchy cobblestone mucosa with longitudinal ulcers and skip areas resembling Crohn's disease**, but lacking transmural inflammation or granulomas.
**Diffuse erythematous patches with superficial exudates mimicking infectious colitis**, caused by *C. difficile* or *E. coli* rather than chronic immune-mediated damage.
**Segmental ischemic necrosis with ulceration and hemorrhagic borders**, presenting as acute vascular injury rather than the continuous mucosal inflammation of ulcerative colitis.
**Lymphocytic colitis with dense intraepithelial lymphocytosis and minimal crypt architectural distortion**, lacking the crypt abscesses or ulceration seen in UC.
**Behçet's disease-associated multiple round ulcers with discrete margins in the terminal ileum**, accompanied by oral/genital ulcers, not confined to the colon.
